ooabap-008.easyUnderstanding: Testable code matlab aisa code jo easily unit test ho sake bina real database ya real SAP system ke. Jaise chef ka recipe test karna — agar recipe properly likhi hai (ingredients separate, steps clear), toh koi bhi test kar sakta hai bina poora restaurant open kiye. Agar sab kuch ek hi pan mein mix kar diya — onion, masala, daal sab ek saath — toh sirf taste karke judge kar sakte ho, step-by-step test nahi ho sakta. ABAP mein bhi yahi hota hai — agar DB call, business logic, aur output sab ek hi method mein hai, toh test karna impossible hai. Solution: ek method ek kaam kare (single responsibility), dependencies bahar se do (inject via constructor), aur database ko interface ke peeche chhupao. Phir test mein real DB ki jagah ek fake 'mock' class dete hain jo test data return karta hai. ABAP Unit framework (CLASS ltc_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING) is process ko support karta hai. ATC — ABAP Test Cockpit — Eclipse mein automatically tests run karta hai plus code quality checks bhi karta hai. Testable code = less bugs in production, faster debugging, confident changes.
ooabap-008.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Interview mein 'testable code' ka matlab sirf unit test likhna nahi hai — yeh design philosophy hai. Teen pillars yaad rakho:

1. Single Responsibility: Ek method sirf ek kaam kare. Agar method 100 lines ki hai aur DB read bhi karti hai, calculation bhi karti hai, message bhi display karti hai — toh woh testable nahi hai. Tode do teen 20-line methods mein.

2. Dependency Injection via Interfaces: Class ke andar CREATE OBJECT lo_db mat karo. Pehle interface banao (lif_db_reader), constructor mein import karo (io_db TYPE REF TO lif_db_reader). Test mein real class ki jagah mock class pass karo jo same interface implement karti hai lekin hardcoded test data return karti hai.

3. ABAP Unit: CLASS ltc_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING. Methods CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT=>ASSERT_EQUALS se verify karte hain. Setup method mein mock inject karo, test method mein result assert karo.

Real project example: maine ek pricing calculator class banayi. Database reader interface ke peeche tha. Unit test mein mock reader inject kiya jo hardcoded price condition data return karta tha — koi real DB hit nahi. Test 2 seconds mein chala, production scenario 100% cover hua.

ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) Eclipse mein transport se pehle run karo — AUnit tests + Code Inspector checks + custom rules sab ek saath. Ye CI/CD equivalent hai ABAP mein. Interviewer ko ye bolna strong impression deta hai.
ooabap-008.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'To make ABAP code testable, I follow three principles: single-responsibility methods, interface-based dependencies with constructor injection, and ABAP Unit test classes. Instead of creating database accessor objects inside methods using CREATE OBJECT, I define an interface for each dependency and inject it through the constructor. This way, unit tests can pass a mock implementation that returns hardcoded test data — no real database needed. I use CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT=>ASSERT_EQUALS for assertions and organize tests in FOR TESTING classes that are excluded from production delivery. Before every transport, I run ATC in Eclipse which executes AUnit tests plus Code Inspector checks automatically.'

Hinglish tip: Agar interviewer pooche 'how do you test without real database?' — Answer: 'I inject a mock implementation of the DB interface in tests, returning test data directly. The real class uses actual DB; the test class uses the mock. This is the core of testable design in ABAP.' Ye ek line sab kuch explain kar deti hai.
ooabap-008.codeExamples:
INTERFACE lif_db_reader.
  METHODS get_price
    IMPORTING iv_matnr TYPE matnr
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_price) TYPE p.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS lcl_pricing DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_db TYPE REF TO lif_db_reader.
    METHODS calculate
      IMPORTING iv_matnr TYPE matnr
                iv_qty   TYPE i
      RETURNING VALUE(rv_total) TYPE p.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_db TYPE REF TO lif_db_reader.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lcl_pricing IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    mo_db = io_db.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD calculate.
    rv_total = mo_db->get_price( iv_matnr ) * iv_qty.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltc_pricing_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS test_calculate FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lcl_mock_db DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_db_reader.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_mock_db IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_db_reader~get_price.
    rv_price = 100.  " Hardcoded test data
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltc_pricing_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_calculate.
    DATA(lo_mock) = NEW lcl_mock_db( ).
    DATA(lo_calc) = NEW lcl_pricing( lo_mock ).
    DATA(lv_result) = lo_calc->calculate( iv_matnr = 'MAT001' iv_qty = 3 ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = lv_result exp = 300 ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
ooabap-008.interviewScriptEnglish: To make ABAP code testable, I follow three principles: single-responsibility methods, interface-based dependencies with constructor injection, and ABAP Unit test classes. Instead of creating database accessor objects inside methods using CREATE OBJECT, I define an interface for each dependency and inject it through the constructor. This way, unit tests can pass a mock implementation that returns hardcoded test data — no real database needed. I use CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT=>ASSERT_EQUALS for assertions and organize tests in FOR TESTING classes that are excluded from production delivery. Before every transport, I run ATC in Eclipse which executes AUnit tests plus Code Inspector checks automatically.
ooabap-008.interviewScriptHinglish: ABAP code ko testable banane ke liye main teen principles follow karta hoon: single-responsibility methods, interface-based dependencies with constructor injection, aur ABAP Unit test classes. Methods ke andar CREATE OBJECT se database accessor objects banane ki jagah, main har dependency ke liye ek interface define karta hoon aur use constructor ke through inject karta hoon. Is tarah unit tests ek mock implementation pass kar sakte hain jo hardcoded test data return karti hai — koi real database nahi chahiye. Main assertions ke liye CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT=>ASSERT_EQUALS use karta hoon aur tests ko FOR TESTING classes mein organize karta hoon jo production delivery se exclude hoti hain. Har transport se pehle Eclipse mein ATC run karta hoon jo AUnit tests plus Code Inspector checks automatically execute karta hai.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Test method ka naam test_ se shuru na karna ya FOR TESTING annotation miss karna — test run hi nahi hoga.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: ABAP Unit framework sirf FOR TESTING classes aur methods ko discover karta hai — bina annotation ke method kabhi execute hi nahi hota, aur test suite green dikhti hai jabki actually kuch test hua hi nahi. Is false confidence ke saath bugs seedhe production tak pahunch jaate hain.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Aur assertions ke bina sirf code execute karna — woh test nahi hai, woh dry run hai.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Mock class mein real DB call karna — woh mock nahi hai.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Test phir system data pe depend karta hai — alag client ya system mein wahi test fail ho jaata hai, runtime seconds se minutes mein chala jaata hai, aur test runs flaky ho jaate hain kyunki data har environment mein different hai.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Test slow hoga aur data-dependent hoga. Mock should always return deterministic, hardcoded values.

commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: ATC static checks ke saath AUnit tests bhi execute karta hai — agar aap ise sirf Code Inspector samjhoge toh tests likhne ki zaroorat hi nahi feel hogi, aur 'ATC green' ka matlab quality samajh ke untested code transport ho jaata hai.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: ATC mein unit tests bhi run hote hain — dono alag alag cheezein nahi hain, ek integrated quality check hai.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Directly CALL FUNCTION rakhe bina wrapper ke aur phir complain karna ki testability nahi hai.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Direct CALL FUNCTION ko mock nahi kiya ja sakta — har unit test real FM execute karta hai jo DB ya RFC hit karta hai. Class effectively unit-testable nahi rehti aur tests slow, data-dependent integration tests ban jaate hain.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Wrapper pattern ek extra class add karta hai but testability dramatically improve hoti hai.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Defines a contract (LIF_DB_READER) that any implementing class must fulfill — enables substitution for tests.
Declares method signatures inside an interface or class — here GET_PRICE returns a packed price.
Header of a class declaring its public, protected, and private sections — testable code starts here.
Special method invoked on NEW — used here to inject the DB-reader dependency.
Declares that this class promises to implement every method of the interface — enables polymorphism.
Class addition that marks the class as an ABAP Unit test class — runs via SE80/SE24 unit test option.
Inline object creation operator (NEW lcl_pricing( lo_mock )) — replaces CREATE OBJECT for ABAP 7.40+.
Standard SAP assertion class for unit tests — provides assert_equals and related methods.
Method parameter style that returns a single value usable in expressions — ideal for functional-style methods.
Reference type modifier — used to type the constructor parameter as a reference to the interface.

ooabap-008.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Writing testable OO ABAP — depend on interfaces, inject collaborators via constructor, mock them in unit tests.

Used for:
- Designing classes that can run in unit tests without a real database or SAP runtime
- Defining narrow interfaces (LIF_DB_READER) that allow mock implementations
- Writing ABAP Unit test classes with FOR TESTING and cl_abap_unit_assert

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- INTERFACE lif_db_reader. METHODS get_price ... ENDINTERFACE.
- METHODS constructor IMPORTING io_db TYPE REF TO lif_db_reader.
- CLASS ltc_... DEFINITION FOR TESTING. cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( exp = ... act = ... ).

Interview keyword: Depend on interface, inject via constructor, mock in test

followupAnswerBank.question: What is ABAP Unit and how do you write a basic test?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ABAP Unit SAP ka built-in testing framework hai. Main FOR TESTING addition ke saath ek test class define karta hoon, FOR TESTING se annotated test methods likhta hoon, aur assertions ke liye CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT=>ASSERT_EQUALS use karta hoon. Tests SE80 ya Eclipse mein run hote hain aur production packages se excluded rehte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: ABAP Unit is SAP's built-in testing framework. I define a test class with the FOR TESTING addition, write test methods annotated FOR TESTING, and use CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT=>ASSERT_EQUALS for assertions. Tests run in SE80 or Eclipse and are excluded from production packages.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real project mein every critical method ke liye at least 2-3 unit tests likho — positive case, negative case (empty input), aur edge case (boundary value). ATC se pre-transport check automatically run hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Test method ka naam test_ se shuru na karna ya FOR TESTING annotation miss karna — test run hi nahi hoga. Aur assertions ke bina sirf code execute karna — woh test nahi hai, woh dry run hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
CLASS ltc_my_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION SHORT.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS test_positive FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS ltc_my_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_positive.
    DATA(lv_result) = lcl_calc=>add( 2 + 3 ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_result  exp = 5
      msg = 'Addition failed' ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is a test double or mock class in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Test double ek lightweight class hai jo ek real dependency jaisa hi same interface implement karti hai par database ya external system ko hit kiye bina hardcoded test data return karti hai. Main test include mein ek local mock class banata hoon, usse class under test mein constructor ke through inject karta hoon, aur output ko CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT use karke verify karta hoon.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A test double is a lightweight class that implements the same interface as a real dependency but returns hardcoded test data without hitting the database or external system. I create a local mock class in the test include, inject it via constructor into the class under test, and verify the output using CL_ABAP_UNIT_ASSERT.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Complex scenarios mein multiple doubles chahiye — ek DB reader mock, ek mailer mock, ek config reader mock. Each returns pre-set data. Test sirf business logic verify karta hai, not infrastructure.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Mock class mein real DB call karna — woh mock nahi hai. Test slow hoga aur data-dependent hoga. Mock should always return deterministic, hardcoded values.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
CLASS lcl_mock_reader DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_db_reader.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_mock_reader IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_db_reader~get_customer.
    rs_customer-kunnr = 'C001'.
    rs_customer-name1 = 'Test Customer'.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) and when do you use it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ATC SAP ka quality gate tool hai jo transport se pehle ABAP Unit tests, Code Inspector checks, aur custom rule sets ek saath run karta hai. Main har significant change ke baad Eclipse mein ATC run karta hoon. Yeh ek pass mein unit test failures, performance anti-patterns, security issues, aur Clean ABAP violations catch karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: ATC is SAP's quality gate tool that runs ABAP Unit tests, Code Inspector checks, and custom rule sets together before transport. I run ATC in Eclipse after every significant change. It catches unit test failures, performance anti-patterns, security issues, and Clean ABAP violations in one pass.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project mein ATC ko transport workflow mein mandatory set karo — ATC fail hone pe transport block ho jaye. Ye manual review reduce karta hai aur regressions pakad leta hai early.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: ATC sirf code inspector samajhna. ATC mein unit tests bhi run hote hain — dono alag alag cheezein nahi hain, ek integrated quality check hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" ATC runs automatically in Eclipse:
" Right-click on object > Run As > ABAP Test Cockpit
" Or configure in SE80 > ABAP Test Cockpit
" Check variants define which rules to apply:
" - SCI_UNIT_TESTS: runs all FOR TESTING methods
" - SCI_FINDING: performance / security checks
" Transport block on ATC failure: configured in STMS

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you test a method that calls a function module?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main function module call ko ek chhoti class mein wrap karta hoon jo ek interface implement karti hai. Class under test yeh wrapper constructor injection ke through receive karti hai. Unit tests mein, main test data return karne wala mock wrapper inject karta hoon. Yeh function module ko business logic tests se isolate karta hai aur real FM execution trigger kiye bina code ko testable banata hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I wrap the function module call in a small class that implements an interface. The class under test receives this wrapper via constructor injection. In unit tests, I inject a mock wrapper returning test data. This isolates the function module from business logic tests and makes the code testable without triggering real FM execution.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Legacy ABAP mein bahut saare FM calls hote hain. Ek-ek ko wrap karna time-consuming hai — prioritize critical business logic wale FM calls first. BAPI wrappers especially important hain kyunki they touch external systems.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Directly CALL FUNCTION rakhe bina wrapper ke aur phir complain karna ki testability nahi hai. Wrapper pattern ek extra class add karta hai but testability dramatically improve hoti hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
INTERFACE lif_bapi_caller.
  METHODS create_order
    IMPORTING is_header TYPE bapisdhd1
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_vbeln) TYPE vbeln_va.
ENDINTERFACE.

CLASS lcl_bapi_wrapper DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_bapi_caller.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_bapi_wrapper IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_bapi_caller~create_order.
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2'
      EXPORTING order_header_in = is_header
      IMPORTING salesdocument   = rv_vbeln.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is RISK LEVEL and DURATION in ABAP Unit?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: RISK LEVEL test impact classify karta hai: HARMLESS bina DB changes wale pure logic tests ke liye, DANGEROUS data modify karne wale tests ke liye, CRITICAL external system interactions ke liye. DURATION expected execution time hint karta hai. Main hamesha unit tests ke liye RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION SHORT use karta hoon, yeh ensure karte hue ki yeh fast aur kisi bhi environment mein including production read-only check systems mein run karne ke liye safe ho.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: RISK LEVEL classifies test impact: HARMLESS for pure logic tests with no DB changes, DANGEROUS for tests that modify data, CRITICAL for external system interactions. DURATION hints at expected execution time. I always use RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION SHORT for unit tests, ensuring they are fast and safe to run in any environment including production read-only check systems.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: ATC configuration mein HARMLESS tests hamesha run hote hain. DANGEROUS tests sirf designated test systems mein run karo. Production pe sirf HARMLESS tests allow hone chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Default RISK LEVEL CRITICAL rakhna — toh tests production pe run nahi hote. Always explicitly declare HARMLESS for unit tests that don't touch real data.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
CLASS ltc_safe_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING
  RISK LEVEL HARMLESS
  DURATION SHORT.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS test_logic FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS ltc_safe_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD test_logic.
    " Pure logic test, no DB access
    DATA(lv_result) = zcl_discount=>calculate( 1000 ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = lv_result exp = 100 ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you structure a class to make it easily testable?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek testable class saari dependencies ko constructor ke through interface references ke roop mein leti hai, business logic ko bina side effects wale chhote pure methods mein rakhti hai, global variables avoid karti hai, aur single responsibility follow karti hai. Har method ko exactly ek cheez karni chahiye aur independently testable hona chahiye. Main data retrieval ko data processing se alag methods mein bhi separate karta hoon taaki unhe isolation mein test kiya ja sake.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A testable class takes all dependencies via constructor as interface references, keeps business logic in small pure methods with no side effects, avoids global variables, and follows single responsibility. Each method should do exactly one thing and be independently testable. I also separate data retrieval from data processing into distinct methods so they can be tested in isolation.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real project mein class design review karo pehle: agar constructor mein CREATE OBJECT dikhe toh red flag. Agar method 80 lines ki hai toh red flag. Agar TABLES mein global data hai toh red flag. Teen red flags = refactor needed before testing.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Ek god class banana jo sab kuch kare — fetch, process, validate, display sab ek class mein. Such classes are impossible to unit test meaningfully.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
CLASS zcl_order_processor DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_db    TYPE REF TO zif_order_db
                io_pricer TYPE REF TO zif_pricer.
    METHODS process
      IMPORTING iv_order_id TYPE vbeln
      RETURNING VALUE(rs_result) TYPE zs_result.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_db    TYPE REF TO zif_order_db.
    DATA mo_pricer TYPE REF TO zif_pricer.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between unit test and integration test in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Unit tests mocked dependencies ke saath ek single class test karte hain — fast, isolated, no real DB access. Integration tests verify karte hain ki components real database ya RFC calls ke saath ek saath kaam karte hain. ABAP mein, main FOR TESTING classes mein HARMLESS risk level ke saath unit tests likhta hoon, aur integration tests ya toh dedicated test systems mein DANGEROUS ABAP Unit tests ke roop mein ya SIT ke dauraan test team dwara run separate test programs ke roop mein.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Unit tests test a single class with mocked dependencies — fast, isolated, no real DB access. Integration tests verify that components work together with real database or RFC calls. In ABAP, I write unit tests in FOR TESTING classes with HARMLESS risk level, and integration tests either as DANGEROUS ABAP Unit tests in dedicated test systems or as separate test programs run by the test team during SIT.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: CI pipeline mein: unit tests har commit pe run karo (30 seconds). Integration tests nightly ya pre-transport run karo (minutes to hours). Dono complementary hain — unit tests bugs early pakadenge, integration tests end-to-end flow verify karenge.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Unit tests mein real SELECT statements rakhna — woh integration tests ban jaate hain, not unit tests. Unit tests should always use mocks.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Unit test (mock DB, HARMLESS)
CLASS ltc_unit DEFINITION FOR TESTING RISK LEVEL HARMLESS.
  " Uses lcl_mock_db — no real DB hit
ENDCLASS.

" Integration test (real DB, DANGEROUS - test system only)
CLASS ltc_integration DEFINITION FOR TESTING RISK LEVEL DANGEROUS.
  " Uses real zcl_order_db — actual SELECT on DB
  " Run only in dedicated test client
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle setup and teardown in ABAP Unit tests?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ABAP Unit SETUP aur TEARDOWN methods provide karta hai jo har test method se respectively pehle aur baad mein run hote hain. Main SETUP ka use mocks aur class under test initialize karne ke liye karta hoon — har test ko independent rakhta hoon. TEARDOWN data modify karne wale DANGEROUS tests ke liye important hai, rollback ya cleanup ensure karta hai. Yeh test pollution rokta hai jahan ek test ki state doosre ko affect karti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: ABAP Unit provides SETUP and TEARDOWN methods that run before and after each test method respectively. I use SETUP to initialize mocks and the class under test — keeping each test independent. TEARDOWN is important for DANGEROUS tests that modify data, ensuring rollback or cleanup. This prevents test pollution where one test's state affects another.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Agar setup mein object create karo aur teardown mein free karo — every test gets a fresh, clean state. Test order dependency eliminate hoti hai. Ek test ka failure doosre tests ko affect nahi karta.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: SETUP use na karna aur instance variables ko first test mein initialize karna — toh tests order-dependent ho jaate hain. Always use SETUP for initialization.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
CLASS ltc_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING RISK LEVEL HARMLESS.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_mock   TYPE REF TO lcl_mock_db.
    DATA mo_sut    TYPE REF TO zcl_processor. " system under test
    METHODS setup.
    METHODS teardown.
    METHODS test_happy_path FOR TESTING.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS ltc_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD setup.
    mo_mock = NEW lcl_mock_db( ).
    mo_sut  = NEW zcl_processor( mo_mock ).
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD teardown.
    CLEAR: mo_mock, mo_sut.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD test_happy_path.
    DATA(lv_result) = mo_sut->process( 'ORD001' ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_not_initial( lv_result ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

ooabap-009.easyUnderstanding: Dependency injection matlab: 'zaroorat ki cheez andar se mat banao — bahar se do.' Jaise cycle chalani hai — tum cycle khud nahi banate, koi deta hai tumhe. Agar class ke andar hi dependency create kari toh woh class tightly coupled hai — class aur dependency dono ek saath hain, alag nahi ho sakte. Aur testing ke waqt problem: real object ban jaata hai, mock nahi de sakte. Lekin agar constructor mein dependency inject ki — bahar se object pass kiya — toh test mein real object ki jagah mock object de sakte ho. Jaise restaurant mein chef ko vegetables khud ugane ki zaroorat nahi — supplier deta hai. Chef sirf pakana jaanta hai. Test mein tum 'test vegetables' doge — same quality, controllable. Yahi dependency injection ka core idea hai. Clean ABAP guide explicitly kehta hai: prefer constructor injection, avoid creating dependencies inside methods.
ooabap-009.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Dependency injection ka concept samajhna important hai kyunki yeh testability, maintainability aur loose coupling teeno ka foundation hai.

Bina DI ke (tightly coupled):
Class ke andar CREATE OBJECT lo_logger karo — ab class logger se permanently bound hai. Agar logger change karna ho ya test mein mock logger chahiye — impossible without changing the class.

DI ke saath (loosely coupled):
Constructor mein io_logger TYPE REF TO lif_logger import karo. Ab bahar se koi bhi object pass ho sakta hai jo lif_logger implement karta ho — real logger, file logger, mock logger, null logger.

Teen types of injection:
1. Constructor Injection (BEST — Clean ABAP preferred): dependency constructor mein import hoti hai. Hamesha visible hai, mandatory hai, immutable hai after construction.
2. Setter Injection: ek SET_LOGGER method se inject karo — optional dependency ke liye useful.
3. Interface Injection: kam use hota hai ABAP mein.

Real project example: maine ek email notification class banayi. Email sender ko interface ke peeche rakha (lif_mailer). Production mein real SMTP mailer inject hota hai. Test mein mock mailer inject hota hai jo sirf list mein add karta hai — koi real email nahi jaata. Same class, different behavior by injection.

S/4HANA mein RAP framework bhi DI-friendly patterns encourage karta hai — behavior implementations mein dependencies injected hoti hain.
ooabap-009.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Dependency injection means passing required dependencies to a class from outside instead of creating them internally. I prefer constructor injection — the dependency is declared as an interface reference in the constructor's IMPORTING parameter. This makes the class loosely coupled because any object implementing the interface can be passed. In unit tests, I inject a mock implementation that returns test data without touching the real database or external system. Clean ABAP guidelines recommend constructor injection as the standard pattern because dependencies are explicit, mandatory, and immutable after object construction.'

Hinglish tip: Interview mein ek simple one-liner yaad rakho — 'Dependency inject karo, andar create mat karo.' Aur testability ke saath connect karo: 'DI ke bina proper unit testing possible nahi hai because you cannot replace real dependencies with mocks.' Ye link strong answer banata hai aur interviewer ko pata chalta hai ki aap practical hain.
ooabap-009.codeExamples:
" Interface for the dependency
INTERFACE lif_logger.
  METHODS log
    IMPORTING iv_message TYPE string.
ENDINTERFACE.

" Real implementation
CLASS lcl_app_logger DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_logger.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_app_logger IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_logger~log.
    " Write to application log (SLG1)
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD' EXPORTING i_msgtext = iv_message.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Class using DI (constructor injection)
CLASS zcl_order_service DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_logger TYPE REF TO lif_logger.  " Injected!
    METHODS process_order
      IMPORTING iv_vbeln TYPE vbeln.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_logger TYPE REF TO lif_logger.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_order_service IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD constructor.
    mo_logger = io_logger.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD process_order.
    mo_logger->log( |Processing order { iv_vbeln }| ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Mock for testing
CLASS lcl_mock_logger DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_logger.
    DATA mt_log TYPE string_table.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_mock_logger IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_logger~log.
    APPEND iv_message TO mt_log.  " Capture, don't write real log
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
ooabap-009.interviewScriptEnglish: Dependency injection means passing required dependencies to a class from outside instead of creating them internally. I prefer constructor injection — the dependency is declared as an interface reference in the constructor's IMPORTING parameter. This makes the class loosely coupled because any object implementing the interface can be passed. In unit tests, I inject a mock implementation that returns test data without touching the real database or external system. Clean ABAP guidelines recommend constructor injection as the standard pattern because dependencies are explicit, mandatory, and immutable after object construction.
ooabap-009.interviewScriptHinglish: dependency injection ka matlab hai required dependencies ko class mein bahar se pass karna, andar create karne ki jagah. Main constructor injection prefer karta hoon — dependency constructor ke IMPORTING parameter mein interface reference ke roop mein declare hoti hai. Yeh class ko loosely coupled banata hai kyunki koi bhi object jo us interface ko implement kare pass ho sakta hai. Unit tests mein main ek mock implementation inject karta hoon jo test data return karti hai bina real database ya external system ko touch kiye. Clean ABAP guidelines constructor injection ko standard pattern recommend karti hain kyunki dependencies explicit, mandatory, aur object construction ke baad immutable hoti hain.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Interface banana lekin fir bhi class ke andar NEW lcl_real_db( ) karna — ye DI nahi hai, ye interface use karna hai tightly coupled way mein.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Class ab bhi concrete implementation se tightly coupled hai — unit test mein mock inject karne ka koi raasta nahi bachta, toh har test real DB hit karta hai. Interface ka asli benefit (substitutability) zero ho jaata hai.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Constructor injection use karo: METHODS constructor IMPORTING io_db TYPE REF TO lif_db_reader — kaunsi implementation aayegi yeh caller ya factory decide kare; class ke andar concrete class ka NEW kabhi nahi.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Setter injection use karna kyunki 'constructor mein bahut saare parameters ho jaate hain.' Agar constructor ke 6+ parameters hain toh class ki responsibility zyada hai — split the class, don't switch to setter.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Setter injection se object partially-initialized state mein ban sakta hai — agar koi setter call bhool gaya toh uninitialized reference dereference hote hi runtime pe OBJECTS_OBJREF_NOT_ASSIGNED dump aata hai. Constructor injection guarantee deta hai ki object kabhi invalid state mein exist nahi karta.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Constructor injection hi rakho; agar constructor 6+ dependencies maang raha hai toh Single Responsibility ke hisaab se class ko chhoti cohesive classes mein split karo, ya closely related dependencies ko ek facade/parameter object mein group karo.

commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Is extreme par har chhoti value object ke liye bhi interface aur injection banane padte hain — codebase unnecessary abstractions se bhar jaata hai, constructors bloat hote hain, aur navigation slow ho jaati hai bina kisi testability gain ke.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Internal value objects aur small helper structures banane ke liye NEW perfectly fine hai. Sirf external dependencies (DB, logger, mailer, RFC) ke liye injection necessary hai.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Ek class mein CASE-WHEN blocks for different behaviors rakhna ('IF lv_type = 'EMAIL' ..
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Har naya type (e.g. WHATSAPP notification) add karne ke liye already-tested class ko phir se modify karna padta hai — har change regression risk lata hai, aur wahi CASE blocks dheere-dheere multiple methods mein duplicate ho jaate hain. Yeh Open-Closed Principle ka classic violation aur maintenance trap hai.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: ELSEIF lv_type = SMS'). Ye OCP violation hai. Alag implementations banao, inject karo.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Defines the abstraction (LIF_LOGGER) that the using class depends on — enables substitution at runtime.
Class declares it implements every method of the interface — both real logger and mock logger do this.
Standard SAP function module that adds a message to the application log (SLG1) — used inside the real logger.
Constructor injection — the dependency is supplied from outside the class instead of created inside.
Reference type pointing to the interface, not a concrete class — keeps the class loosely coupled.
Private member that stores the injected dependency for later use by process_order.
Standard table of strings used by the mock logger to capture log messages for assertion.
Mock logger captures messages into an internal table for the test to verify after the action.
Application log transaction — destination of BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD writes in the real logger implementation.

ooabap-009.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Dependency Injection in ABAP — pass collaborators via constructor instead of instantiating them inside the class.

Used for:
- Decoupling business classes from concrete loggers, DB readers, RFC clients
- Replacing real dependencies with mocks in unit tests (lcl_mock_logger captures messages)
- Following the Dependency Inversion Principle in Clean ABAP

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- METHODS constructor IMPORTING io_logger TYPE REF TO lif_logger.
- DATA mo_logger TYPE REF TO lif_logger. mo_logger = io_logger.
- Real impl uses BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD writing to SLG1; mock impl appends to string_table for asserts

Interview keyword: Constructor injection plus interface dependency plus mockable in test

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between tight coupling and loose coupling in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Tight coupling ka matlab hai ki ek class directly ek concrete class par depend karti hai — dependency change karne ke liye class ko khud change karna padta hai. Loose coupling ka matlab hai ek interface par depend karna — koi bhi implementation class ko modify kiye bina substitute ki ja sakti hai. ABAP mein, main loose coupling achieve karta hoon saari external dependencies ke liye interfaces define karke aur unhe constructor ke through inject karke.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Tight coupling means a class directly depends on a concrete class — changing the dependency requires changing the class itself. Loose coupling means depending on an interface — any implementation can be substituted without modifying the class. In ABAP, I achieve loose coupling by defining interfaces for all external dependencies and injecting them via constructor.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project mein tight coupling production incidents ka bada reason hai — ek class change ki, 5 doosri classes break ho gayin. Loose coupling se blast radius chhota rehta hai. Design review mein pehla check: koi bhi CREATE OBJECT without interface?
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Interface banana lekin fir bhi class ke andar NEW lcl_real_db( ) karna — ye DI nahi hai, ye interface use karna hai tightly coupled way mein.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Tight coupling (BAD)
CLASS lcl_service DEFINITION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_db TYPE REF TO lcl_real_db.  " Concrete!
ENDCLASS.
" Constructor: mo_db = NEW lcl_real_db( ).  ← Cannot mock!

" Loose coupling (GOOD)
CLASS lcl_service DEFINITION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_db TYPE REF TO lif_db.  " Interface!
ENDCLASS.
" Constructor: mo_db = io_db.  ← Any implementation works!

followupAnswerBank.question: What is constructor injection vs setter injection in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Constructor injection dependency ko ek mandatory constructor parameter ke roop mein pass karta hai — uske bina object instantiate nahi ho sakta, jisse dependency explicit aur immutable ban jaati hai. Setter injection construction ke baad ek optional dependency set karne ke liye ek dedicated method use karta hai. Clean ABAP constructor injection ko default ke roop mein recommend karta hai kyunki dependencies hamesha visible aur hamesha set hoti hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Constructor injection passes the dependency as a mandatory constructor parameter — the object cannot be instantiated without it, making the dependency explicit and immutable. Setter injection uses a dedicated method to set an optional dependency after construction. Clean ABAP recommends constructor injection as the default because dependencies are always visible and always set.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: 90% cases mein constructor injection use karo. Setter injection sirf wahan use karo jahan dependency truly optional hai (like an optional debug logger) ya jahan circular dependency force karta hai setter use karne ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Setter injection use karna kyunki 'constructor mein bahut saare parameters ho jaate hain.' Agar constructor ke 6+ parameters hain toh class ki responsibility zyada hai — split the class, don't switch to setter.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Constructor injection (mandatory dep)
METHODS constructor
  IMPORTING io_db     TYPE REF TO lif_db
            io_logger TYPE REF TO lif_logger.

" Setter injection (optional dep)
METHODS set_tracer
  IMPORTING io_tracer TYPE REF TO lif_tracer.
" Usage:
lo_service = NEW zcl_service( io_db = lo_db io_logger = lo_log ).
lo_service->set_tracer( lo_tracer ).  " Optional

followupAnswerBank.question: Why is CREATE OBJECT inside a method considered an anti-pattern?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek method ke andar NEW ya CREATE OBJECT use karke dependency create karna concrete implementation ko hardcode karta hai, jisse usse tests ya different deployment contexts mein substitute karna impossible ho jaata hai. Yeh ek anti-pattern hai kyunki yeh Dependency Inversion Principle violate karta hai — high-level logic ko low-level concrete classes par depend nahi karna chahiye. Fix yeh hai ki dependency ko constructor ke through interface reference ke roop mein inject karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Creating a dependency inside a method using NEW or CREATE OBJECT hardcodes the concrete implementation, making it impossible to substitute in tests or in different deployment contexts. It is an anti-pattern because it violates the Dependency Inversion Principle — high-level logic should not depend on low-level concrete classes. The fix is to inject the dependency via constructor as an interface reference.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Code review mein: agar method ke andar CREATE OBJECT dikhe aur woh object ek external system ya DB access karta hai — flag it. Immediate refactor candidate. Business logic mein VALUE OBJECTS banane ke liye NEW use karna acceptable hai (like NEW zcl_price_calculation( )) — dependency nahi hai woh.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Har NEW ko bad samajhna. Internal value objects aur small helper structures banane ke liye NEW perfectly fine hai. Sirf external dependencies (DB, logger, mailer, RFC) ke liye injection necessary hai.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Anti-pattern: hardcoded dependency
METHOD process.
  DATA(lo_db) = NEW zcl_real_db( ).  " Cannot mock in tests!
  DATA(lt_data) = lo_db->read( iv_id ).
ENDMETHOD.

" Correct: injected dependency
METHOD constructor.
  mo_db = io_db.  " Passed from outside
ENDMETHOD.
METHOD process.
  DATA(lt_data) = mo_db->read( iv_id ).  " Mockable!
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does DI support the Open/Closed Principle in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Dependency injection Open/Closed Principle ko support karta hai kyunki new behavior add karne ke liye interface ki ek nayi implementation banani padti hai, existing class modify nahi karni padti. Example, agar mujhe SMS alerts ki jagah email alerts chahiye, toh main ek naya lcl_email_notifier banata hoon jo lif_notifier implement kare aur usse inject karta hoon — service class unchanged rehti hai. Yeh system ko modification ke bina extensible banata hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Dependency injection supports the Open/Closed Principle because adding new behavior requires creating a new implementation of the interface, not modifying the existing class. For example, if I need email alerts instead of SMS alerts, I create a new lcl_email_notifier implementing lif_notifier and inject it — the service class remains unchanged. This makes the system extensible without modification.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real project example: notification system banaya tha. Initially sirf SAP inbox notification thi. Client ne email bhi maanga — naya class banaya, interface implement kiya, inject kiya. Original service class: zero changes. OCP perfectly applied.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Ek class mein CASE-WHEN blocks for different behaviors rakhna ('IF lv_type = 'EMAIL' ... ELSEIF lv_type = SMS'). Ye OCP violation hai. Alag implementations banao, inject karo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
INTERFACE lif_notifier.
  METHODS notify IMPORTING iv_msg TYPE string.
ENDINTERFACE.

" New implementation — no change to service class
CLASS lcl_email_notifier DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_notifier.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_email_notifier IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_notifier~notify.
    " Send email via SCOT/SO_NEW_DOCUMENT_ATT_SEND_API1
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
" Inject: lo_service = NEW zcl_alert_service( NEW lcl_email_notifier( ) ).

followupAnswerBank.question: Can you explain DI with a real SAP project scenario?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek pricing engine project mein, price data business unit ke hisaab se standard condition tables, ek custom Z-table, ya ek external REST service se aa sakta tha. Maine ek lif_price_reader interface define kiya jisme ek single get_price method tha. Teen classes ne usse implement kiya. Runtime par, ek factory configuration ke based correct implementation inject karta tha. Unit tests fixed test prices return karne wale mock reader use karte the — actual data sources ke saath zero coupling.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In a pricing engine project, price data could come from standard condition tables, a custom Z-table, or an external REST service depending on the business unit. I defined a lif_price_reader interface with a single get_price method. Three classes implemented it. At runtime, a factory injected the correct implementation based on configuration. Unit tests used a mock reader returning fixed test prices — zero coupling to actual data sources.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Factory pattern DI ke saath well works karta hai — factory decides which concrete class to instantiate based on config/parameter. Caller sirf interface jaanta hai. Ye pattern large enterprise ABAP systems mein bahut use hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Factory bhi tightly couple karna — factory mein bhi hardcoded class names. Factory ko configuration-driven banao (config table se class name read karo, RTTI se instantiate karo) for true flexibility.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Factory pattern with DI
CLASS zcl_price_factory DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS get_reader
      IMPORTING iv_source TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(ro_reader) TYPE REF TO lif_price_reader.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_price_factory IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_reader.
    CASE iv_source.
      WHEN 'STANDARD'. ro_reader = NEW zcl_kondp_reader( ).
      WHEN 'CUSTOM'.   ro_reader = NEW zcl_ztable_reader( ).
      WHEN 'REST'.     ro_reader = NEW zcl_rest_reader( ).
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does DI relate to the SOLID principles in ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Dependency injection directly Dependency Inversion Principle implement karta hai — high-level business logic abstractions (interfaces) par depend karti hai, concrete classes par nahi. Yeh existing classes ko modify kiye bina new implementations allow karke Open/Closed ko bhi enable karta hai, aur Liskov Substitution ko support karta hai kyunki koi bhi interface implementation safely substitute ki ja sakti hai. Clean ABAP saare SOLID principles promote karta hai aur DI unka practical enabler hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Dependency injection directly implements the Dependency Inversion Principle — high-level business logic depends on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete classes. It also enables Open/Closed by allowing new implementations without modifying existing classes, and supports Liskov Substitution since any interface implementation can be safely substituted. Clean ABAP promotes all SOLID principles and DI is their practical enabler.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Interview mein SOLID ka ek line summary: 'S-single responsibility, O-open/closed, L-liskov substitution, I-interface segregation, D-dependency inversion. DI is how D is practically implemented in ABAP.' Ye connected thinking interviewer ko impress karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: SOLID principles sirf theory mein jaanna aur practical ABAP code mein link na karna. Always connect principle to a concrete ABAP pattern: DI = D principle, small methods = S principle.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" D - Dependency Inversion: depend on interface, not concrete
CLASS zcl_billing DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_tax TYPE REF TO lif_tax_calc.  " Interface, not lcl_vat!
ENDCLASS.
" L - Liskov: any implementation works
lo_billing = NEW zcl_billing( NEW zcl_vat_calc( ) ).   " EU tax
lo_billing = NEW zcl_billing( NEW zcl_gst_calc( ) ).   " India tax
" O - Open/Closed: add zcl_us_tax_calc — billing class unchanged

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the null object pattern and how does it relate to DI?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Null object pattern ek class banata hai jo ek interface implement karti hai par apne methods mein kuch nahi karti. DI context mein, yeh optional dependencies ke liye ek safe default implementation ke roop mein kaam karta hai. Har call se pehle IS BOUND check karne ke bajaye, main default ke roop mein ek null object inject karta hoon — yeh interface ko satisfy karta hai par koi side effects produce nahi karta. Example, ek null logger silently messages discard karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The null object pattern creates a class that implements an interface but does nothing in its methods. In DI context, it serves as a safe default implementation for optional dependencies. Instead of checking IS BOUND before every call, I inject a null object by default — it satisfies the interface but produces no side effects. For example, a null logger silently discards messages.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production code mein IS BOUND checks se code ugly hota hai. Null object se: hamesha inject karo (real ya null), calling code mein check nahi chahiye. Framework initialize karte waqt null objects as defaults set karo.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Optional dependency ke liye null check (IF mo_logger IS BOUND. mo_logger->log( ). ENDIF.) everywhere rakhna — code clutter hai. Null object inject karo, check hata do.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Null logger — does nothing
CLASS lcl_null_logger DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES lif_logger.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS lcl_null_logger IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD lif_logger~log.
    " Intentionally empty — swallows log calls safely
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
" Usage with optional logger:
lo_service = NEW zcl_service(
  io_db     = lo_real_db
  io_logger = COND #( WHEN lo_logger IS BOUND
                      THEN lo_logger
                      ELSE NEW lcl_null_logger( ) ) ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle circular dependencies in ABAP OO?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ABAP OO mein circular dependencies usually ek design problem signal karti hain — do classes bahut tightly related hain aur ya toh merge honi chahiye ya restructure honi chahiye. Technical fix setter injection hai: pehle A construct karo, A constructor mein inject karke B construct karo, phir B inject karne ke liye A ka setter call karo. Lekin, real fix yeh hai ki ek teesra interface ya class extract kiya jaaye jis par dono depend karte hon, cycle ko todte hue.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Circular dependencies in ABAP OO usually signal a design problem — two classes are too tightly related and should either be merged or restructured. The technical fix is setter injection: construct A first, construct B with A injected via constructor, then call A's setter to inject B. However, the real fix is to extract a third interface or class that both depend on, breaking the cycle.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Circular dependency production mein runtime error cause kar sakti hai ya Stack Overflow dump. Design review mein circular dependency immediately refactor karo — yeh architecture smell hai. Dependency graph draw karo, cycle identify karo, break the cycle.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Circular dependency ko setter injection se 'fix' karna bina root cause solve kiye. Setter injection is a band-aid. The real solution is always to redesign the dependency graph.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Circular: A needs B, B needs A — BAD
" Solution: extract common interface
INTERFACE lif_event_source.
  METHODS get_events RETURNING VALUE(rt_events) TYPE ztt_events.
ENDINTERFACE.
" A depends on interface, B implements interface
" No circular dependency — both depend on abstraction
CLASS zcl_processor DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_source TYPE REF TO lif_event_source.
ENDCLASS.

ooabap-010.easyUnderstanding: Clean ABAP matlab SAP ka official open-source guide jo kehta hai: 'aisa code likho jisko kal koi aur bhi samajh sake — bina tumse pooche.' Jaise clean kitchen — ingredients labeled hain, tools proper jagah hain, koi mess nahi. Agar koi bhi cook aaye toh recipe follow kar sake. Code mein bhi: meaningful names (lv_customer_name not lv_cn1), chhote methods (ek kaam karo), no global mess, proper exceptions. Kyun? Kyunki code ek baar likha jaata hai lekin hazaron baar padha jaata hai — maintenance mein zyada time lagta hai writing mein nahi. Clean ABAP rules GitHub pe public hain, ATC automatically check karta hai. Interview mein Clean ABAP mention karna batata hai ki aap modern ABAP jaante hain, sirf old-school code nahi likhte.
ooabap-010.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Clean ABAP ke 10 most important rules jo interview mein bolne chahiye:

1. Self-documenting names: lv_customer_name not lv_cn1. No Hungarian notation (lv_, ls_, lt_ zaroori hain type ke liye lekin abbreviations avoid karo).

2. Methods do ONE thing: 20-40 lines max. Agar method zyada badi hai toh extract karo.

3. Early return pattern: agar condition fail ho toh turant RETURN karo — deep nesting avoid hota hai.

4. Prefer RETURNING over EXPORTING: single output ke liye RETURNING cleaner hai.

5. Exception classes not MESSAGE: MESSAGE type 'E' is old-style. Raise EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_my_error.

6. NEW over CREATE OBJECT: modern syntax hai.

7. No global variables: sab kuch parameters se pass karo.

8. Comment the WHY not the WHAT: 'Rounding needed for FI posting' — not 'Divide by 100.'

9. Prefer functional style: COND #( ), SWITCH #( ), FILTER #( ), VALUE #( ) for readability.

10. ATC in Eclipse: SAP ne Clean ABAP rules ATC mein integrate kiye hain — Eclipse mein run karo, violations automatically detect hoti hain.

Real project example: purana ABAP program tha 800 lines ki ek FORM routine mein. Maine refactor kiya — 20 chhote methods, meaningful names, early returns, exception classes. Result: next developer ne 2 ghante mein bug fix kiya jo pehle 2 din leta tha.
ooabap-010.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Clean ABAP is SAP's open-source coding guideline available on GitHub. Key principles I follow in my projects: methods do one thing and stay within 20-40 lines, names are self-descriptive without cryptic abbreviations, I use early returns to avoid deeply nested IF blocks, prefer RETURNING over EXPORTING for single outputs, raise exception classes instead of MESSAGE statements, and avoid global variables by passing data through parameters. ATC in Eclipse automatically checks many Clean ABAP rules before transport, so I run it as part of my development workflow.'

Hinglish tip: Interview mein ek powerful one-liner: 'Clean ABAP ka goal hai ki code ko uske author ke bina koi bhi samajh sake aur safely modify kar sake.' Phir ek concrete example do — 'main methods ko max 20-30 lines mein rakhta hoon aur early return pattern use karta hoon deep nesting avoid karne ke liye.' Concrete example without prompt = strong candidate signal.
ooabap-010.codeExamples:
" ===== BAD CODE (pre-Clean ABAP style) =====
FORM process_cust USING lv_cn TYPE kunnr
                        lv_fl TYPE c
                  CHANGING lv_rc TYPE i.
  DATA: lv_d TYPE kna1.
  IF lv_cn <> space.
    IF lv_fl = 'X'.
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM kna1 INTO lv_d
        WHERE kunnr = lv_cn.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        IF lv_d-land1 = 'DE'.
          lv_rc = 1. " German customer
        ELSE.
          lv_rc = 2.
        ENDIF.
      ELSE.
        MESSAGE 'Not found' TYPE 'E'. " Old style error
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

" ===== CLEAN CODE (Clean ABAP style) =====
METHOD get_customer_region.
  IF iv_customer_id IS INITIAL.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_invalid_input
      EXPORTING mv_message = 'Customer ID must not be empty'.
  ENDIF.

  SELECT SINGLE land1 FROM kna1
    WHERE kunnr = @iv_customer_id
    INTO @DATA(lv_country).

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_customer_not_found
      EXPORTING mv_customer_id = iv_customer_id.
  ENDIF.

  rv_region = SWITCH #( lv_country
    WHEN 'DE' THEN zif_regions=>german
    WHEN 'US' THEN zif_regions=>north_america
    ELSE            zif_regions=>other ).
ENDMETHOD.
ooabap-010.interviewScriptEnglish: Clean ABAP is SAP's open-source coding guideline available on GitHub. Key principles I follow in my projects: methods do one thing and stay within 20-40 lines, names are self-descriptive without cryptic abbreviations, I use early returns to avoid deeply nested IF blocks, prefer RETURNING over EXPORTING for single outputs, raise exception classes instead of MESSAGE statements, and avoid global variables by passing data through parameters. ATC in Eclipse automatically checks many Clean ABAP rules before transport, so I run it as part of my development workflow.
ooabap-010.interviewScriptHinglish: Clean ABAP SAP ki open-source coding guideline hai jo GitHub par available hai. Main apne projects mein yeh key principles follow karta hoon: methods ek hi cheez karein aur 20-40 lines mein rahein, names self-descriptive ho bina cryptic abbreviations ke, deep nested IF blocks avoid karne ke liye early returns use karoon, single outputs ke liye EXPORTING ki jagah RETURNING prefer karoon, MESSAGE statements ki jagah exception classes raise karoon, aur global variables avoid karke parameters ke through data pass karoon. Eclipse mein ATC bahut saare Clean ABAP rules transport se pehle automatically check karta hai, isliye main use apne development workflow ka part bana ke chalata hoon.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Early return ke baad bhi ELSE rakhna — woh early return ka faida khatam karta hai.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: ELSE block ek unnecessary nesting level add karta hai — guard clause ke baad code linear hona chahiye, lekin ELSE reader ko do parallel branches suggest karta hai. Readability girti hai aur future edits aksar galat branch mein land hote hain.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Agar early return hai toh ELSE ki zaroorat nahi.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Exception class banao lekin andar MESSAGE statement rakho — double error handling.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: MESSAGE TYPE 'E' classical message handling trigger karta hai — background job ya RFC context mein program implicitly terminate ho sakta hai aur caller ka CATCH block kabhi reach hi nahi hota. Do parallel error mechanisms debugging ko unpredictable bana dete hain.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Exception class mein meaningful attributes rakho aur MESSAGE avoid karo completely.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Method ko chhota karna sirf for the sake of size — har ek line ek method banana.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Logic dozens of one-line methods mein bikhar jaata hai — ek simple flow samajhne ke liye 10 navigation jumps lagte hain aur debugger ka call stack unreadable ho jaata hai. Fragmentation bhi utna hi bada readability problem hai jitna 200-line method.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Balance chahiye: too large = too complex, too small = fragmented logic that's hard to follow.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Meaningful naam dene ke chakkar mein bahut lamba naam: lv_the_customer_number_from_the_kna1_table_for_billing.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Itne lambe names lines wrap karate hain aur signal-to-noise ratio girate hain — aur ABAP ki 30-character name limit aise names ko truncate ya inconsistent abbreviations mein force kar deti hai, jisse naming aur bhi confusing ho jaati hai.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Balance: lv_customer_number is perfect. Self-explaining but not verbose.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Procedural subroutine syntax — Clean ABAP discourages it in new code; methods preferred.
OOP method body — Clean ABAP standard for new code with descriptive naming.
Raises a class-based exception (zcx_invalid_input) — replaces old MESSAGE TYPE 'E' for control flow.
Custom exception class for invalid input — signals contract violation cleanly to the caller.
Custom exception class raised when KNA1 returns SY-SUBRC <> 0 — preserves business meaning.
Reads single field with inline @ host variable — Clean ABAP avoids reading full structures unnecessarily.
Inline data declaration with @ escape — concise and Clean ABAP-approved.
Constructor expression that returns a value based on conditions — replaces verbose IF/ELSE chains.
Interface constant access via =>; constants centralized in interfaces (Clean ABAP pattern).
Predicate that tests for empty/null values — used as guard in clean validation method.

ooabap-010.quickRevisionNotes:
What: Clean ABAP principles — meaningful names, methods over FORMs, class-based exceptions, inline DATA, modern constructor expressions.

Used for:
- Rewriting legacy FORM-based code into testable METHOD-based code
- Replacing MESSAGE TYPE 'E' with RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE for control flow
- Using SWITCH/COND/VALUE/REDUCE constructor expressions and inline DATA(...) for clarity

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- METHOD get_customer_region. SELECT SINGLE land1 FROM kna1 WHERE kunnr = @iv_customer_id INTO @DATA(lv_country). ENDMETHOD.
- RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_invalid_input EXPORTING mv_message = ...
- rv_region = SWITCH #( lv_country WHEN 'DE' THEN ... ELSE ... ).

Interview keyword: Clean ABAP — methods, exceptions, inline DATA, expressions

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the early return pattern in Clean ABAP and why is it preferred?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Early return pattern ka matlab hai method ke shuru mein preconditions ya failure cases check karna aur agar woh fail hote hain toh immediately return ho jaana, main logic ko nested IF blocks mein wrap karne ke bajaye. Clean ABAP isse recommend karta hai kyunki yeh nesting depth kam karta hai, happy path ko left margin par rakhta hai, aur guard conditions ke through scroll kiye bina method ke main intent ko clearly visible banata hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The early return pattern means checking preconditions or failure cases at the start of a method and returning immediately if they fail, instead of wrapping the main logic in nested IF blocks. Clean ABAP recommends this as it reduces nesting depth, keeps the happy path at the left margin, and makes the method's main intent clearly visible without scrolling through guard conditions.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real code review tip: agar method mein 4+ levels of nesting dikh rahe hain (IF inside IF inside IF inside LOOP) — refactor with early returns. Extract nested blocks into small methods. Nesting depth is a direct readability indicator.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Early return ke baad bhi ELSE rakhna — woh early return ka faida khatam karta hai. Agar early return hai toh ELSE ki zaroorat nahi.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Deep nesting (BAD)
IF iv_id IS NOT INITIAL.
  IF lo_db IS BOUND.
    DATA(ls_rec) = lo_db->read( iv_id ).
    IF ls_rec IS NOT INITIAL.
      process( ls_rec ).  " Main logic buried deep
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.

" Early return (CLEAN)
IF iv_id IS INITIAL. RETURN. ENDIF.
IF lo_db IS NOT BOUND. RETURN. ENDIF.
DATA(ls_rec) = lo_db->read( iv_id ).
IF ls_rec IS INITIAL. RETURN. ENDIF.
process( ls_rec ).  " Main logic at top level

followupAnswerBank.question: Why does Clean ABAP prefer exception classes over MESSAGE statements?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: MESSAGE statements ko programmatically handle karna mushkil hai — yeh ya toh user ko display hote hain ya processing abort karte hain, caller ke paas intercept aur recover karne ka koi tarika nahi hota. Exception classes caller ko specific exceptions CATCH karne, error attributes inspect karne, unhe log karne, aur decide karne dete hain ki kaise aage badhna hai. Yeh testable bhi hain — ABAP Unit exceptions catch kar sakta hai aur ASSERT_RAISES se unhe verify kar sakta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: MESSAGE statements are difficult to handle programmatically — they either display to the user or abort processing, with no way for the caller to intercept and recover. Exception classes allow the caller to CATCH specific exceptions, inspect error attributes, log them, and decide how to proceed. They are also testable — ABAP Unit can catch exceptions and verify them with ASSERT_RAISES.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Migration tip: purane programs mein MESSAGE TYPE 'E' bahut dikhega. Refactor plan: ek zcx_ exception class banao, MESSAGE ki jagah RAISE EXCEPTION rakho, callers mein TRY-CATCH add karo. Step by step migrate karo, ek method at a time.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Exception class banao lekin andar MESSAGE statement rakho — double error handling. Exception class mein meaningful attributes rakho aur MESSAGE avoid karo completely.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Old style (BAD)
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Order not found' TYPE 'E'.  " Hard to test, no recovery
ENDIF.

" Clean ABAP (GOOD)
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE zcx_order_not_found
    EXPORTING mv_vbeln = iv_vbeln.  " Caller can CATCH and decide
ENDIF.

" Caller:
TRY.
  lo_svc->get_order( iv_vbeln ).
CATCH zcx_order_not_found INTO DATA(lx_ex).
  " Log and continue, don't crash
ENDTRY.

followupAnswerBank.question: What does 'methods do one thing' mean in practice?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek method ek cheez tab karta hai jab woh single level of abstraction par operate karta hai aur usse AND use kiye bina ek verb se describe kiya ja sakta hai. Practice mein, main methods ko 30 lines ke neeche rakhta hoon. Agar ek method data fetch karta hai, validate karta hai, aur display ke liye format karta hai, toh main usse teen methods mein split karta hoon: read_customer, validate_customer, aur format_for_display. Har ek independently testable aur reusable hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A method does one thing when it operates at a single level of abstraction and can be described with one verb without using AND. In practice, I keep methods under 30 lines. If a method fetches data, validates it, and formats it for display, I split it into three methods: read_customer, validate_customer, and format_for_display. Each is independently testable and reusable.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Code review shortcut: read method name, then read code. Agar code naam se match nahi karta ya naam mein AND chahiye — split needed. Method names should be contracts.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Method ko chhota karna sirf for the sake of size — har ek line ek method banana. Balance chahiye: too large = too complex, too small = fragmented logic that's hard to follow.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAD: One huge method doing everything
METHOD process_order.  " 150 lines: fetch, validate, price, post, notify
ENDMETHOD.

" CLEAN: Each method one responsibility
METHOD process_order.
  DATA(ls_order) = read_order( iv_vbeln ).       " Fetch
  validate_order( ls_order ).                    " Validate (raises on fail)
  DATA(lv_price) = calculate_price( ls_order ).  " Price
  post_to_fi( iv_vbeln = ls_order-vbeln iv_amount = lv_price ).
  notify_customer( ls_order-kunnr ).
ENDMETHOD.  " 8 lines, clearly readable

followupAnswerBank.question: How does Clean ABAP treat variable naming conventions?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Clean ABAP standard prefixes (local variable ke liye lv_, structure ke liye ls_, table ke liye lt_, object ke liye lo_) allow karta hai kyunki yeh ABAP convention hai. Lekin, yeh cryptic abbreviations discourage karta hai — lv_cust_no, lv_cn se better hai, aur lv_customer_number aur bhi better hai. Standard prefixes ke aage type-indicating suffixes avoid karo. Test: kya koi variable naam padh kar uska matlab bina comment ke samajh sakta hai?
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Clean ABAP allows standard prefixes (lv_ for local variable, ls_ for structure, lt_ for table, lo_ for object) as they are ABAP convention. However, it discourages cryptic abbreviations — lv_cust_no is better than lv_cn, and lv_customer_number is even better. Avoid type-indicating suffixes beyond standard prefixes. The test: can someone read the variable name and understand its meaning without a comment?
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Legacy ABAP naming nightmares: lv_a, lv_b, lv_x, lv_temp everywhere. Refactoring pehla step: meaningful rename. ABAP Rename Refactoring (Ctrl+R in Eclipse) safely renames all occurrences. 30 minutes of renaming can save days of debugging.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Meaningful naam dene ke chakkar mein bahut lamba naam: lv_the_customer_number_from_the_kna1_table_for_billing. Balance: lv_customer_number is perfect. Self-explaining but not verbose.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAD naming
DATA: lv_n  TYPE kunnr,
      lv_d  TYPE d,
      lv_fl TYPE c.
SELECT SINGLE kunnr FROM kna1 INTO @lv_n WHERE land1 = 'DE'.

" CLEAN naming
DATA: lv_customer_id   TYPE kunnr,
      lv_posting_date  TYPE d,
      lv_is_vip_active TYPE abap_bool.
SELECT SINGLE kunnr FROM kna1
  INTO @lv_customer_id
  WHERE land1 = 'DE'.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between commenting the WHAT vs the WHY in Clean ABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Clean ABAP kehta hai code achhe naming ke through self-explanatory hona chahiye, isliye comments ko yeh explain nahi karna chahiye ki code kya karta hai — yeh redundant hai. Comments ko WHY explain karna chahiye: business reasons, non-obvious constraints, historical context, ya regulatory requirements. Example, 'divide by 100 — legacy table stores amounts in minor currency units' jaisa comment why explain karta hai, what nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Clean ABAP says code should be self-explanatory through good naming, so comments should not explain what the code does — that is redundant. Comments should explain WHY: business reasons, non-obvious constraints, historical context, or regulatory requirements. For example, a comment like 'divide by 100 — legacy table stores amounts in minor currency units' explains the why, not the what.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Code review: delete all 'what' comments and see if code is still understandable. If yes — great code. If no — improve naming. Only add 'why' comments for genuinely non-obvious business logic. Less comments = more readable code when names are good.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Commenting every line to 'be thorough.' It clutters the code. Outdated comments are worse than no comments — they mislead. Good names + targeted why-comments = best combination.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAD: commenting the WHAT
lv_amount = lv_amount / 100.  " Divide by 100

" GOOD: commenting the WHY
" Legacy FI interface stores amounts in minor currency units (cents).
" Convert to base currency unit before BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST call.
lv_amount_eur = lv_amount_cents / 100.

" GOOD: no comment needed — name is self-explanatory
DATA lv_is_vat_applicable TYPE abap_bool.
lv_is_vat_applicable = COND #( WHEN ls_customer-land1 = 'DE' THEN abap_true
                                ELSE abap_false ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How does ATC enforce Clean ABAP rules automatically?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Eclipse mein ATC configured check variants ke through Clean ABAP rules enforce karta hai jisme SAP standard checks aur custom organizational rules dono shamil hote hain. Yeh method length, naming conventions, obsolete statements ka use, missing SY-SUBRC checks, SELECT * usage, aur kai aur cheezein check karta hai. Main ATC ko transport requests release hone se pehle automatically run karne ke liye configure karta hoon, agar critical violations exist karti hain toh transport block karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: ATC in Eclipse enforces Clean ABAP rules through configured check variants that include both SAP standard checks and custom organizational rules. It checks method length, naming conventions, use of obsolete statements, missing SY-SUBRC checks, SELECT * usage, and more. I configure ATC to run automatically before transport requests are released, blocking transport if critical violations exist.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: ATC setup steps: SE80 mein global check variant configure karo (or Eclipse settings). ABAP Test Cockpit perspective open karo. Run on object, package, or transport request. Findings mein priority dekho — Error level findings mandatory fix hain, Warning level review karo.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: ATC sirf ek baar run karna project end mein — findings bahut ho jaati hain. Har development cycle mein run karo — small clean = manageable. Big bang cleanup = nightmare.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" ATC checks these Clean ABAP violations automatically:
" 1. Method too long (>40 lines) → finding: reduce method size
" 2. SELECT * without field list → finding: specify fields
" 3. FORM routine usage → finding: use methods instead
" 4. sy-subrc not checked after SELECT → finding: always check
" 5. Obsolete statements (MOVE, COMPUTE) → use = directly

" In Eclipse:
" Right-click on ZCL_MY_CLASS > Run As > ABAP Test Cockpit
" Or: Project > Properties > ABAP Test Cockpit > Auto-run on save

followupAnswerBank.question: Why does Clean ABAP prefer functional ABAP expressions like COND, SWITCH, VALUE?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Clean ABAP functional expressions — COND, SWITCH, VALUE, FILTER, REDUCE — promote karta hai kyunki yeh multiple procedural statements ke bajaye ek expression mein intent ko concisely express karte hain. Yeh intermediate variables ki sankhya kam karte hain, related logic ko ek saath rakhte hain, aur ek nazar mein padhne mein aasaan hote hain. Yeh DATA( ) aur FINAL( ) wale inline declarations ke saath bhi achhe se kaam karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Clean ABAP promotes functional expressions — COND, SWITCH, VALUE, FILTER, REDUCE — because they express intent concisely in one expression instead of multiple procedural statements. They reduce the number of intermediate variables, keep related logic together, and are easier to read at a glance. They also work well with inline declarations using DATA( ) and FINAL( ).
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Migration tip: value-equality CASE/IF chains ko SWITCH se replace karo (WHEN ke baad sirf literal/constant values), aur range ya comparison conditions (>, <, BETWEEN) ke liye COND use karo. LOOP AT with APPEND to another table ko FILTER se replace karo. Ek-ek refactor se code shorter aur clearer hota jaata hai. Eclipse refactoring tools help karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Over-using functional expressions for complex logic — ek nested COND inside REDUCE inside FILTER — becomes unreadable. Functional expressions best for simple-to-medium complexity. Complex logic mein explicit methods aur variables clearer hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Old style (procedural)
DATA lv_status TYPE string.
IF ls_order-netwr > 10000.
  lv_status = 'HIGH'.
ELSEIF ls_order-netwr > 1000.
  lv_status = 'MEDIUM'.
ELSE.
  lv_status = 'LOW'.
ENDIF.

" Clean ABAP (functional) — use COND for range conditions.
" SWITCH needs literal values after WHEN, so it does not fit range checks.
DATA(lv_priority) = COND string(
  WHEN ls_order-netwr > 10000 THEN 'HIGH'
  WHEN ls_order-netwr > 1000  THEN 'MEDIUM'
  ELSE 'LOW' ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you refactor legacy ABAP code to follow Clean ABAP principles?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main regressions avoid karne ke liye legacy ABAP ko incrementally refactor karta hoon. Pehle, main safety net ke roop mein existing behavior ke liye unit tests likhta hoon. Phir main clarity ke liye variables rename karta hoon, large subroutines ko small focused methods mein extract karta hoon, FORM routines ko class methods se replace karta hoon, MESSAGE ki jagah exception classes add karta hoon, aur finally interfaces aur dependency injection introduce karta hoon. ATC violations flag karke har step ko guide karta hai. Har change chhota, tested, aur separately transported hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I refactor legacy ABAP incrementally to avoid regressions. First, I write unit tests for existing behavior as a safety net. Then I rename variables for clarity, extract large subroutines into small focused methods, replace FORM routines with class methods, add exception classes instead of MESSAGE, and finally introduce interfaces and dependency injection. ATC guides each step by flagging violations. Each change is small, tested, and transported separately.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Real project refactoring story template: 'Maine ek 600-line FORM routine ko refactor kiya. Pehle unit tests likhe existing output ke liye. Phir 15 chhote methods extract kiye. Meaningful names diye. ATC clean. Result: next defect fix 4 ghante instead of 2 din.' Numbers ke saath story = credible answer.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Refactoring aur new features ek saath karna — ek commit mein dono. Always separate: refactor commit alag, feature commit alag. Mixing makes code review and rollback impossible.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Legacy: one giant FORM
FORM process USING lv_id TYPE kunnr CHANGING lv_rc TYPE i.
  " 200 lines: fetch, validate, calculate, post, notify
ENDFORM.

" Step 1: Extract to class method
METHOD process.
  DATA(ls_customer) = fetch_customer( iv_id ).   " Extracted
  validate_customer( ls_customer ).              " Extracted
  DATA(lv_amount) = calculate_balance( ls_customer ). " Extracted
  post_balance( iv_kunnr = iv_id iv_amount = lv_amount ).
ENDMETHOD.  " Clean, readable, testable
